coredata.dc:title: An europium functionalized carbon dot-based fluorescence test paper for visual and quantitative point-of-care testing of anthrax biomarker
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Jing
Li, Daquan
Qiu, Yu
Liu, Xinyue
Huang, Liang
Wen, Huimin
Hu, Jun
coredata.dc:description: We reported Eu(III) functionalized carbon dots (CDs-Eu) as a novel ratiometric fluorescent probe for point-of-care testing (POCT) of dipicolinic acid (DPA), a biomarker of anthrax. The probe was synthesized simply by coordination interaction between Eu(III) ions and the CDs with carboxyl and amino groups. Upon addition of DPA, the intrinsic red luminescence of Eu(III) ions can be sensitized due to the presence of energy transfer when DPA chromophore coordinated with Eu(III) ions. The fluorescence peaking at 530 nm as a reference remained essentially constant, leading to ratiometric ﬂuorescence response toward DPA. This approach exhibited a linearity range from 0.5 nM to 5 μM and a detection limit of 0.8 nM. More Importantly, the combination of probe-based test paper and smartphone provided a convenient approach for visual POCT of DPA with a detection limit of 67 nM, which was lower than other reported test paper for DPA detection. The color of fluorescence gradually changed from green to red as the DPA increases, which can be captured by a smartphone and further quantitatively analyzed with a built-in Color Analyzer App. The satisfactory result of detecting DPA accurately and reliably in real samples by the test paper certified its practicality in POCT of DPA.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dipicolinic acid (DPA)
Anthrax biomarker
Europium(III) functionalized carbon dots
Ratiometric
Test paper
POCT

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the visual test paper for DPA assay

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM and particle size distribution of CDs. (B) The emission spectra of the CDs under different excitation wavelengths ranging from 280 to 480 nm. (C) UV–vis absorption (red line), FL excitation (black line) and emission spectra (blue line) of the CDs (solid line) and probe (dash line). Inset: photographs of the probes without (left) and with (right) 30 μM DPA under a 254 nm UV lamp. (D) Zeta potentials of CDs and probe in water. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) C1s spectrum, (B) N1s spectrum, (C) O1s spectrum of the CDs. (D) XPS survey spectrum of the CDs and probe. (E) C1s spectrum, (F) N1s spectrum, (G) O1s spectrum and (H) Eu 3d spectrum of the probe.

(A) FL spectra, (B) ratiometric calibration plot (FL intensity at 615 nm/FL intensity at 530 nm) and (C) CIE chromaticity diagram showing the (x, y) FL color coordinates of probe for various concentrations of DPA. Probe: 24 μg mL−1; pH: 10.5 (10 mM NaOH–NaHCO3 buffer); excitation: 280 nm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) Selectivity and anti-interference ability of probes for DPA over other interferents (2–6, 5 μM, 7–20, 50 μM) in NaOH–NaHCO3 buffer (pH 10.5). 1, Control; 2,Na+; 3, K+; 4, Ca2+; 5, Mg2+; 6, Al3+; 7,Cl−;8, PO4 3−; 9, HCO3 −; 10, SO42−; 11, L-Cys; 12, GSH; 13, Vc; 14, BA; 15, CA; 16, ATP; 17, UTP; 18,CTP; 19, GTP; 20, ppGpp. (B) Selectivity and anti-interference ability of probes for DPA over homologous compounds (50 μM) in NaOH–NaHCO3 buffer (pH 10.5).

The application of the visual detection of DPA with increased concentrations of 0, 1, 5, 10, 20, 30 and 50 μM under a 254 nm UV light excitation by the test paper and a smartphone, in which the R/G values were confirmed by a Color Analyzer App. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration of the ratiometric fluorescence probe for the detection of DPA.
Detection of DPA in real samples (n = 5) by probe and test paper.

head.ce:title: An europium functionalized carbon dot-based fluorescence test paper for visual and quantitative point-of-care testing of anthrax biomarker

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Engineering, Zhejiang University of Technology, Hangzhou, 310014, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemical Engineering, Zhejiang University of Technology, Hangzhou, 310014, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We reported Eu(III) functionalized carbon dots (CDs-Eu) as a novel ratiometric fluorescent probe for point-of-care testing (POCT) of dipicolinic acid (DPA), a biomarker of anthrax. The probe was synthesized simply by coordination interaction between Eu(III) ions and the CDs with carboxyl and amino groups. Upon addition of DPA, the intrinsic red luminescence of Eu(III) ions can be sensitized due to the presence of energy transfer when DPA chromophore coordinated with Eu(III) ions. The fluorescence peaking at 530 nm as a reference remained essentially constant, leading to ratiometric ﬂuorescence response toward DPA. This approach exhibited a linearity range from 0.5 nM to 5 μM and a detection limit of 0.8 nM. More Importantly, the combination of probe-based test paper and smartphone provided a convenient approach for visual POCT of DPA with a detection limit of 67 nM, which was lower than other reported test paper for DPA detection. The color of fluorescence gradually changed from green to red as the DPA increases, which can be captured by a smartphone and further quantitatively analyzed with a built-in Color Analyzer App. The satisfactory result of detecting DPA accurately and reliably in real samples by the test paper certified its practicality in POCT of DPA.

A novel fluorescent test paper was constructed for visual and quantitative POCT of DPA.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Anthrax is a critical infectious disease caused by bacillus anthracis that mainly exists in the form of a spore [1,2]. The spore which is a potential bioterrorism weapon with ‘high stability, can survive under a rugged environment [2–4]. And inhaling more than 104 spores will cause death within 36 h [2,5]. However, infectious and deadly anthrax is arduous to diagnose instantly, because it takes 1–60 days for anthrax symptoms to appear in body. Only accurate and rapid diagnosis of anthrax can timely rescue the infected patients and prevent the widespread outbreak of such catching diseases. Recent research show anthrax has emerged as a serious disease decimating living creature with devastating effects on animal husbandry and eco-tourism in Tanzania and Koraput, Odisha, India [6,7]. In order to prevent and control of anthrax disease and bioterrorism, it is urgent to develop a rapid, sensitive and selective assay for point-of-care testing (POCT) of such spores with simple operation [8,9], especially after the anthrax attacks of 2001 in the USA [10,11].

Dipicolinic acid (DPA), accounting for 5–15% of dry mass of the spore, has been adopted as an unique biomarker for anthrax [1–5,8–14]. So far, Several analytical methods have been used for the detection of DPA including surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy [15], high performance-liquid chromatography [16], polymerase chain reaction [17], fluorescence (FL) techniques [1–5,8–14,18–21]. Among them, FL techniques for DPA detection have increasingly attracted researchers owing to their outstanding merits such as high sensitivity, rapid response time and simplicity [5,18] The high sensitivity and fast response time are excellent advantages that other methods cannot match that make it feasible for POCT. To date, lanthanide containing materials dominate optic probes for DPA detection owing to several features [19]: The salient binding affinity of DPA toward lanthanide ions (especially Eu(III) and Tb(III)), together with the outstanding photophysical properties of Ln–DPA complexes involving sharp, bright emission, large stokes shifts and long FL lifetime endow Ln-based materials with rapid, highly sensitive and selective assay of DPA [1,5,19]. Nonetheless, It is noted the FL intensity of lanthanides is known to be sensitive to external conditions, such as excitation, preparation process, instrument parameters, environmental and target species [20,21]. And fluorescent assays based on lanthanide containing material are still conducted with expensive instruments, and tedious sample operation with professional skills in lab. Considering the challenges mentioned above, the POCT of DPA by lanthanide-based probes is still strait.

In recent years, FL test paper provides an alternative technology for improving POCT and disease diagnosis [22–24]. It has especially attractive owing to the highlight advantages: (1) test papers with low cost, easy operation and portable feasibility can be prepared readily by assembling or printing FL probes into a piece of paper-based substrates. (2) quantitative detection can be realized for real-time analysis and in-time diagnosis by combining smartphone with FL test paper, which smartphone act as a signal reader with installed programs provided by the third-party service facilitator. (3) Numerous method have been developed recently for chemical modification of papers, therefore, introducing different functionalities to meet the needs of POCT is convenient [24]. However, there still has some challenges waiting for researchers to conquer. For example, reducing the cost of reagents and sample processing is unremitting pursuit for expanding the application scope of FL test paper-based POCT [22]. And the sensitivity of FL test paper is always unsatisfactory, mainly because many FL test papers have only one signal, and the target analyte is detected only by the change in FL intensity [25,26]. Ratiometric fluorescent technology can solve the latter problem, it rely on analyte-induced changes in the intensity ratio of two or three emission bands [27–31], accompanied by changes in hue [28]. Both of the change in intensity ratio and hue can be read out by smartphone with installing programs, the result not only greatly increases sensitivity and improves quantification but also endow FL test papers with visual assays by naked eye [28,32]. Besides, owing to its background-free and self-calibrating merits, ratiometric fluorescent technology become an excellent method for reducing interference from external conditions [33–36].

Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), defined by characteristic sizes of <10 nm, have emerged as a charming material in diverse fields of analytical chemistry due to their numerous advantageous properties including water solubility, low toxicity, ease of modification, economic scale-up production, and gifted conjugation with other nanoparticles [37,38]. Currently, although some CDs-based ratiometric fluorescent probes for DPA sensing with high sensitivity and selectivity have been reported [2,5,8,9,13], The combination of ratiometric FL probes and test paper for DPA sensing is still rare [5], and the sensitivity of reported FL test paper is unsatisfactory. Moreover, the reference FL of these probes is generally in the blue region which restrict naked eye detection of DPA. Because compared with red and green FL, human eyes are less sensitive to blue FL [39,40].

Addressed herein, green fluorescent CDs with carboxy and amino groups were prepared by microwave synthesis using citric acid monohydrate and urea as precursor. Then, CDs-Eu as a novel ratiometric DPA probe was obtained by simple mixing between CDs and Eu(III) ions. The probe not only possessed superior water-solubility and stability, but also showed high selectivity and sensitivity for DPA sensing with rapid response. As shown in Scheme 1 , the emission peak at 530 nm of the probe in aqueous solution remained unchanged, thus serving as an internal reference; while the emission peak at 615 nm of Eu(III) ions exhibited sharply enhancement upon binding with DPA, thus serving as the response signal. Consequently, the ratio of two FL intensities gradually changed corresponding to the concentrations of DPA, which can be easily observed by consecutive color change-based visual determination under an UV lamp. Furthermore, a FL paper sensor was prepared based on the probe which can render fast, visual and quantitative POCT of DPA without the aid of large equipment. The test paper was successfully applied for quantitative and visual assay of DPA in river water and orange juice samples, which proved its practical application value.

All chemicals were commercially available unless noted otherwise. EuCl3·6H2O was purchased from J&K Scientific LTD. DPA was offered by Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). Citric acid monohydrate, urea, Vitamin C (Vc), Citric acid (CA), Benzoic acid (BA), nicotinic acid (NA), picolinic acid (PA), isonicotinic acid (ISA), phthalate (PH) were bought from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. Absorbent paper was obtained from Shanghai Jieyi Biotechnology Co., Ltd. All the experiments were carried out in NaOH–NaHCO3 buffer solution (pH = 10.5) unless noted otherwise.

FL emission and excitation spectra of all samples were obtained by a Hitachi F-2700 spectrofluorometer (Tokyo, Japan). Absorption spectra was recorded on a TU-1900 UV–vis spectrometer (Beijing Purkinje General Instrument CO, LTD China). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra was measured using a Bruker model VECTOR22 Fourier-transform spectrometer (Bruker Corporation, Germany). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis was performed on FEI-F20 electron microscope operated at 200 kV (FEI Corporation, USA). The zeta potential was performed on a Zetasizer (Malvern NanoZS Instrument). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out using an AXIS Ultra DLD photoelectron spectrometer (Kratos Corporation, Japan).

The CDs which contains carboxyl groups and amino groups were prepared based on previous report with some modifications [41,42]. Firstly, citric acid monohydrate (3 g) and urea (6 g) were completely dissolved in 10 mL water. Then, the solution was heated in a 450 W microwave oven for 3–4 min until a complete evaporation of water occurs and dark-brown powder was obtained. The crude products were then purified with a silica column chromatography (200–300 mesh) using mixtures of methanol and methylene chloride (volume ratio 5:1) as eluents. After removing solvents and further drying under vacuum, the CDs were finally obtained.

Aqueous CDs (10 mL, 0.6 mg mL−1) solution was mixed with 5 mL of aqueous EuCl3 solution (0.1 M) for 3 h at room temperature. The resulting mixture was then dialyzed with a cellulose ester dialysis membrane (MWCO: 100 D) for 2 days to remove“free” Eu(III) ions. A small amount of agglomerated particles were produced and separated by centrifugation (3000 r min−1, 10 min) attributed to the strong coordination between the CDs and Eu(III) ions. The resulting supernatant was dried by lyophilization to obtain probes, then obtained probes were redispersed in water (1 mg mL−1) for further characterization and use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the visual test paper for DPA assay
ce:section.ce:para: Absorbent paper was used to prepare the test paper for detecting DPA. A common hole puncher (hole diameter is about 6 mm) was used to produce circular strips with the same size. The strips were immersed in a dispersion of the probe solution (about 300 μg mL−1) about 5 min, after taking it out of the probe solution, it was naturally dried in the air, the test paper was prepared. Then, 50 μL DPA solution with different concentrations (0, 1, 5, 10, 20, 30 and 50 μM) was dropped into the manufactured test paper, The FL color of the test paper can be observed visually under the irradiation of a 254 nm UV lamp in a dark case. FL photos were taken with a smartphone. The R and G data (RGB color model) of the photos can be quickly obtained using the smartphone (Huawei P30) with a Color Analyzer App. Through data processing, a standard curve can be easily obtained. Substituting R/G value of a sample into standard linear equation, (using the calculator in the smartphone) the concentration of DPA in this sample can be obtained.

Our nanoprobe designed for ratiometric fluorescent detection of DPA was outlined in Scheme 1. The CDs with a emission peaking at 530 nm was chosen as a platform and reference signal, Eu(III) ions were selected as a response signal due to its obvious advantages over Tb (III) ions in a matter of selectivity [43], exclusion of second-order scattering interference and long emission wavelength at the red end of the visible spectrum [4]. The cost of the probe was estimated to be about $0.08742 per milligram including electricity, materials and reagents fee, which was inexpensive.

As shown in Fig. 1 A, the TEM image showed the green-emissive CDs were typical spherical structure with a diameter ranging from 2.2 to 5.8 nm, and an average size of 3.49 nm. The CDs showed excitation-independent emission property and the maximum emission could be available at 530 nm with the maximum excitation at 420 nm (Fig. 1B). The immovability of the emission wavelength was beneficial for applications in chemosensing and bioimaging [2]. As displayed in Fig. S1, the absorption bands at 3462 and 3214 cm−1 were corresponded to O–H bonds and the –NH2 group [2], The peaks located at 1719, 1604, 1060 and 1400 cm−1 were attributed to the CO [44], CC [45], C–O [2], and amido CO–N bond [46]. The results revealed that there were carboxyl and amino groups on the CDs, which endowed the CDs with potential for coordinating with Eu(III) ions. Fig. 1C showed the as-obtained CDs in aqueous solution exhibited three absorption peaks in 249, 327 and 410 nm, probably corresponding to the π→π*, n→σ*, n→π* transition respectively [47]. After the CDs coordinated with Eu(III) ions, the UV absorption spectrum and excitation spectrum changed obviously, and the half peak width of emission peak became larger. Meanwhile, FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S1, red curve) of probe showed significant difference with the original CDs of the O–H, CO,C–O bond and –NH2 group, which is due to the change of dipole moments when Eu(III) ions chelate with CO and N–H bands [48]. Moreover, the zeta potentials (Fig. 1D) showed that the CDs were considerably negatively charged, while the probe displayed positive potential due to the introduction of Eu(III) ions. As shown in Fig. 2 A–D, the CDs were comprised of C, N, O elements with anatomic ratio of C/N/O = 65.48/13.81/20.71. The C1s spectrum of CDs showed five peaks at 284.5, 285.3, 286.2, 287.8, and 289.3 eV (Fig. 2A) that were attributed to sp2 C, sp3 C, C–N, C–O, and C = N/C = O, respectively [42]. The N1s spectrum of CDs showed two peaks at 399.9, and 401.8 eV (Fig. 2 B), which were attributed to the N–C, and N–H bands, respectively [5,42]. The O1s spectrum of CDs showed two peaks at 531.6 and 532.3 eV (Fig. 2C), which were attributed to the C = O, C–OH/C–O–C bands, respectively [49]. It can been seen from Fig. 2D–H, compared to the CDs, the C1s, N1s, O1s spectrum of the probe changed obviously, and a Eu 3d spectrum appeared showed two peaks at 1134.9 and 1164.7 eV, attributed to Eu 3d5/2 and Eu 3d3/2 [5]. In addition, the probe was comprised of C, N, O, Eu elements with an atomic ratio of C/N/O/Eu = 64.83/7.59/26.16/1.43. Apparently, the probe had more oxygen atom than the CDs, this was because the probe contained europium ions, and each europium ion can coordinate with three water molecules [5,11]. Based on the above analysis, it was speculated that the average amount of chelated Eu(III) on every CDs was 1.885. All the above results indicated the probe (CDs-Eu) had been successfully prepared. What is more, after 90 min consecutive illuminations under UV light, the FL intensity ratio (I 615/I 530) was unchanged, indicating the good photochemical stability of the nanoprobe (Fig. S2). The storage stability of the probe and test paper were also investiged, as shown in Fig. S3, our probe had satisfactory stability within 40 days. Fig. S4. demonstrated the test paper possessed good stability within 30 days.

The effects of different reactive conditions (the type of buffer, pH and reactive time) on the detection of DPA were investigated to achieve excellent sensitivity. We firstly investigated the influence of the type of buffer and pH value on the DPA detection (Fig. S5). It is known the FL intensity of Eu(DPA)3 3− is stronger than that of Eu(DPA)+ and Eu(III) ions, DPA exists in the form of H2DPA at low pH, and it changes into HDPA‾ and DPA2− as pH value increases [44]. Eu-DPA complex formed at a 1:3 M ratio of Eu to DPA has a utmost FL intensity [48]. Therefore, as the pH of Tris-HCl buffer increased from 6.5 to 10, the changes of FL intensity ratio (Δ(I 615/I 530)) in the presence and absence of DPA gradually increased. As for NaOH–NaHCO3 buffer, with the increase of pH from 8.5 to 11.5, the alteration of FL intensity ratio increased from 8.5 to 10.5 and reached the maximum value (17.73) at the pH of 10.5, then fell with the pH value increasing attributed to the formation of Eu(III) ions hydroxide [10]. Similarly, As the pH of Britton-Robison buffer increased from 3.0 to 11.5, the changes increased firstly and then decreased. The above results showed that pH 10.5 of NaOH–NaHCO3 buffer was recommended as the optimal pH value for further detection. As shown in Fig. S6, after the addition of 5 μM DPA, the enhancing process levelled off after 15 s with the extension of the incubation time, demonstrating the detection process was very rapid.

DPA not only can acts as the antennas group to absorb the excitation light based on antenna effect but also can substitute the water molecules coordinated with Eu(III) ions to block the nonradiative quenching effect of water molecules [5,11]. As presented in Fig. S7, In the presence of 5 μM DPA, the excitation peak (λem = 615 nm) of Eu(III) ions (1 mM) varied from 264 nm to 277 nm, and peak intensity increased 6.58 times. Meanwhile, the corresponding emission peak intensity at 615 nm of Eu3+ ions (λex = 280 nm) increased 10.66 times. Thanks to the carboxyl group and amino group on the CDs, our probe possessed enough Eu(III) ions, which afford the probe could bind with a lot of DPA. Therefore, Our probe may have a higher sensitivity than Eu(III) ions [2]. As demonstrated in Fig. 3 A, In the presence of 5 μM DPA, the emission peak (615 nm) intensity of probe increased 22.96 times, which confirmed our speculation. The emission spectrum of the nanoprobe prior to DPA addition was dominated by the bands of CDs, with the major emission peak at 530 nm. Addition of a small amount of aqueous DPA solution, the intensity of the emission peak at 530 nm remained unchanged, meanwhile, the emission intensity of Eu(III) ions enhanced obviously due to efficient energy transfer from the DPA ligands to Eu(III) ions [21,36]. And five emission bands were centered at 542, 592, 615, 647, 691 nm under 280 nm excitation, the main transition of the Eu(III) ions (615 nm) excited states 5D0→7F2. Fig. 3B showed that the FL intensity ratio (I 615/I 530) of the probe was proportional to the concentration of DPA in the range of 0.5 nM–5 μM. The limit of detection (LOD) was determined to be as low as 0.8 nM (LOD = 3Sb/m), where m is the slope of the calibration curve and Sb is the standard deviation of the blank. In addition, the emission color change of probe in the presence of DPA can be identified by the naked eye (Fig. 1C), and the amount of DPA can be directly read out from the color change of the CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram (Fig. 3C).

The DPA-induced FL enhancement of probe was highly specific. As shown in Figs. 4A and B, 5
μM common cations (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Al3+), 50 μM common anion ions (Cl−, PO4 3−, HCO3− and SO42−), reductants (L-Cys, GSH and Vc), BA CA, and nucleotides (ATP, CTP, UTP, GTP, ppGpp) were selected to estimate the effect of interference substance on the determination of DPA. And 50 μM homologous compounds (NA, PA, PH and ISA) were used to evaluate the selectivity and anti-interference of the probe. The obtained result indicated the FL intensity ratio (I 615/I 530) of probe was nearly unchanged when adding interferents and homologous compounds separately compared with the control group. Due to the excellent selectivity and anti-interference ability, the probe had great potential to be used for detecting DPA in complex samples.

In response to the demand of a portable and affordable method for visual and quantitative POCT of DPA. A portable and low-cost test paper was developed in this work. As shown in Fig. 5, under a 254 nm UV lamp, the FL color of test paper underwent an obvious change from green to red through light yellow in the presence of DPA with different concentrations, which can be easily recognized by the naked eye. A smartphone installed with a Color Analyzer App (Fig. S8) was applied to show the red-green-blue (RGB) intensity of the obtained photos, ensuring the test paper accessible for POCT application. The intensity ratio of R/G was calculated to evaluate the FL difference of the test paper in the presence of different concentrations of DPA. According to the intensity ratio of R/G, a calibration linearity curve of R/G = 0.74163 + 0.04989 [DPA] could be obtained for the DPA determination range from 1.0 to 30 μM. The LOD was found to be 67 nM. This was much more sensitive than the infectious dose (60 μM) spores [8]. And compared to other reported test papers, our test paper possessed excellent sensitivity (Table S1). All the above results indicated the test paper has a potential application for visual and quantitative POCT of DPA due to its simplicity and high sensitivity.

Different real samples including river water and orange juice samples were taken to appraise the feasibility of the ratiometric FL probe and test paper for determination of DPA. River water samples were obtained from a local river (Cao e river) and orange juice was purchased from supermarket. These samples were pretreated with ordinary qualitative filter paper and 0.22 μm membrane to remove the insoluble substances. Then, different concentrations of standard DPA solutions were added into the probe solution (DPA: 0.03, 0.3, 3 μM) and test paper (DPA: 3, 20 μM) containing 10% real samples as listed in Table 1 . And each measurement was done in quintuplicate. The analytical results indicated the recoveries of DPA in the range of 97.45%–103.94% with RSD of around 0.16%–3.92% for river water samples and 96.77%–105.0% with RSD of around 0.17%–4.70% for orange juice samples. The acceptable results verified a practicability in deteching DPA accurately and reliably by the probe and test paper.

In summary, we prepared a new ratiometric fluorescent probe for Bacillus anthrax biomarker sensing. The probe (CDs-Eu) used the CDs as a scaffold for coordination with Eu(III) ions and a luminescent reference. DPA could enhance the FL of Eu(III) ions on the surface of CDs due to efficient energy enhancements of Eu(III) ions with increasing concentration of DPA while the emission intensity of CDs at 530 nm remained unchanged. The developed approach showed sensitive responses with a linearity range from 0.5 nM to 5 μM and a LOD of 0.8 nM. More importantly, a test paper was constructed for visual and quantitative POCT of DPA by the change of the fluorescent color using a smartphone installed with a program Color Analyzer App as a detection platform. The linearity range and detection limit of the test paper are 1–30 μM and 67 nM, the detection limit is lower than other reported test paper for DPA detection. And spiked tests results suggesting it has potential application in the field of public safety. This work reveals an effective method for visual and quantitative POCT of an anthrax biomarker.

Jing Wang: Design, Methodology, Writing-Reviewing and Editing. Daquan Li.: Experiment, Data curation, Writing-Original draft preparation. Yu Qiu: Experiment, Software. Xinyue Liu: Investigation, Experiment. Liang Huang: Software, Reviewing. Huimin Wen: Supervision. Jun Hu: Writing-Reviewing and Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing ﬁnancial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (21875218, 51872261 and U1909214) and Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province (LR19E020002).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2020.121377.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multiporous terbium phosphonate coordination polymer microspheres as fluorescent probes for trace anthrax biomarker detection
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.Q. Luo, L. Zhang, L.Y. Zhang, B.H. Yu, Y.J. Wang, W.B. Zhang, Multiporous terbium phosphonate coordination polymer microspheres as fluorescent probes for trace anthrax biomarker detection, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 11 (2019) 15998-16005.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Anthrax biomarker: an ultrasensitive fluorescent ratiometry of dipicolinic acid by using terbium(III)-modified carbon dots
Talanta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.L. Liu, B.B. Chen, J.H. He, C.M. Li, Y.F. Li, C.Z. Huang, Anthrax biomarker: An ultrasensitive fluorescent ratiometry of dipicolinic acid by using terbium(III)-modified carbon dots, Talanta 191 (2019) 443-448.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metal complex as an optical sensing platform for rapid multimodal recognition of a pathogenic biomarker in real-life samples
ACS Sustain. Chem. Eng.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Dey, D. Biswakarma, S. Bhattacharya, Metal complex as an optical sensing platform for rapid multimodal recognition of a pathogenic biomarker in real-life samples, ACS Sustain. Chem. Eng. 7 (2019) 569-577.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Eriochrome black T-Eu3+ complex as a ratiometric colorimetric and fluorescent probe for the detection of dipicolinic acid, a biomarker of bacterial spores
Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.D. Yilmaz, H.A. Oktem, Eriochrome black T-Eu3+ complex as a ratiometric colorimetric and fluorescent probe for the detection of dipicolinic acid, a biomarker of bacterial spores, Anal. Chem. 90 (2018) 4221-4225.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A ratiometric fluorescence visual test paper for an anthrax biomarker based on functionalized manganese-doped carbon dots
Sensor. Actuator. B Chem.
